en.extract:
The El Oro parakeet (Pyrrhura orcesi) is an Endangered species of bird in subfamily Arinae of the family Psittacidae, the African and New World parrots. It is endemic to Ecuador.


Taxonomy and systematics
The El Oro parakeet is monotypic. Its closest relative is believed to be the maroon-tailed parakeet (P. melanura).


Description
The El Oro parakeet is 22 to 24 cm (8.7 to 9.4 in) long and weighs 65 to 75 g (2.3 to 2.6 oz). Adult males have a mostly green head with a red forehead and lores. Their upperparts are green. Their throat, the sides of their neck, and their breast are green with grayish buff bars giving an indistinct scaly appearance. The rest of their underparts are green with some faint dull reddish on the belly. Their wing is mostly green with red primary coverts; their primaries are blue. Their tail is maroon with a green base and a dusky maroon underside. Their bill is dusky and their iris is dark with bare pinkish skin surrounding it. Females are almost the same as males but with much reduced red on the forehead. Immature birds are similar to adults but with even less red on the head and wing and none on the belly.


Distribution and habitat
The El Oro parakeet is found only on the west slope of the Ecuadoran Andes in Azuay and El Oro provinces. It is known only from an area of about 3,800 km2 (1,500 sq mi) that is 5 to 10 km (3 to 6 mi) wide and 100 km (60 mi) long. It inhabits humid subtropical and tropical forest mostly between 600 and 1,300 m (2,000 and 4,300 ft) of elevation but has been found as low as 300 m (1,000 ft) and as high as 1,800 m (5,900 ft).


Behavior


Movement
The El Oro parakeet is not known to have any pattern of movements.


Feeding
The El Oro parakeet feeds on fruit and berries; some known sources are figs (Ficus), Heliocarpus, Hieronyma, and Cecropia. It typically forages in flocks of up to about 15 individuals but flocks as large as 60 have been observed.


Breeding
The El Oro parakeet's breeding season appears to span from March to July. It nests in natural tree cavities that have been noted as high as 24 m (80 ft) above the ground. It uses a variety of trees but Dacroide peruviana appears to be preferred. It also uses nest boxes provided by Buenaventura Reserve. El Oro parakeets breed cooperatively, a breeding pair being accompanied by up to six helpers.


Vocalization
The El Oro parakeet's most common call is "a series of grating notes with a rather hoarse quality, e.g. "crreeet crreeet crreeet" " that is given both from a perch and in flight. Perched birds also call with single "chuk" or "krrr" notes, though perched birds are often silent. Members of a flock "call frequently and simultaneously, producing a noisy chattering."


Status
The IUCN originally assessed the El Oro parakeet as Vulneralble but since 2000 has listed it as Endangered. It has a very limited range and an estimated population of fewer than 1000 mature individuals. "Remaining habitat is fragmented, and both range and population are thought to be declining." The Buenaventura Reserve was created in 1999 by Fundación de Conservación Jocotoco to conserve this species and the El Oro tapaculo (Scytalopus robbinsi). It encompasses about 4,000 ha (15 sq mi) of cloudforest in which the Fundación maintains a network of nest boxes. About 75% of the El Oro parakeet's population is found there and nearby.


References


Further reading
Schaefer, H. M., and Veronika Schmidt. Final Report El Oro Parakeet Project, Southwestern Ecuador 2002–2003. Rep. Google Docs. Web. 1 May 2010. <https://web.archive.org/web/20110719031618/http://www.fjocotoco.org/pdf/final-report-pyrrhura.pdf>.

The '''El Oro parakeet''' ('''''Pyrrhura orcesi''''') is an [[Endangered species (IUCN status)|Endangered]] species of bird in subfamily [[Arinae]] of the family [[Psittacidae]], the African and New World parrots. It is [[endemic]] to [[Ecuador]].<ref name=IOC13.1>{{cite web |url=https://www.worldbirdnames.org/new/bow/parrots/ |title=Parrots, cockatoos |website=IOC World Bird List |version =v 13.1 |editor-last1=Gill |editor-first1= F. |editor-last2=Donsker|editor-first2=D.|editor-last3=Rasmussen |editor-first3=P. |date=January 2023 |access-date=18 February 2023 }}</ref>

==Taxonomy and systematics==

The El Oro parakeet is [[monotypic]].<ref name=IOC13.1/> Its closest relative is believed to be the [[maroon-tailed parakeet]] (''P. melanura'').<ref name=EOPA-BOW>Collar, N. and P. F. D. Boesman (2020). El Oro Parakeet (''Pyrrhura orcesi''), version 1.0. In Birds of the World (J. del Hoyo, A. Elliott, J. Sargatal, D. A. Christie, and E. de Juana, Editors). Cornell Lab of Ornithology, Ithaca, NY, USA. https://doi.org/10.2173/bow.elopar1.01 retrieved March 12, 2023</ref>

==Description==

The El Oro parakeet is {{convert|22|to|24|cm|in|abbr=on}} long and weighs {{convert|65|to|75|g|oz|abbr=on}}. Adult males have a mostly green head with a red forehead and [[lore (anatomy)|lores]]. Their upperparts are green. Their throat, the sides of their neck, and their breast are green with grayish buff bars giving an indistinct scaly appearance. The rest of their underparts are green with some faint dull reddish on the belly. Their wing is mostly green with red [[Primaries (birds)|primary]] [[Covert feather|coverts]]; their primaries are blue. Their tail is maroon with a green base and a dusky maroon underside. Their bill is dusky and their iris is dark with bare pinkish skin surrounding it. Females are almost the same as males but with much reduced red on the forehead. Immature birds are similar to adults but with even less red on the head and wing and none on the belly.<ref name=EOPA-BOW/><ref name=Ridgely2>{{cite book | last =Ridgely | first =Robert S. | last2 =Greenfield | first2 =Paul J. | title =The Birds of Ecuador: Field Guide | publisher =Cornell University Press | volume = II| date =2001 | location =Ithaca | page =189 | isbn =978-0-8014-8721-7 }}</ref>

==Distribution and habitat==

The El Oro parakeet is found only on the west slope of the Ecuadoran Andes in [[Azuay Province|Azuay]] and [[El Oro Province|El Oro]] provinces. It is known only from an area of about {{convert|3800|km2|sqmi|abbr=on}} that is {{convert|5|to|10|km|mi|0|abbr=on}} wide and {{convert|100|km|mi|-1|abbr=on}} long. It inhabits humid subtropical and tropical forest mostly between {{convert|600|and|1300|m|ft|-2|abbr=on}} of elevation but has been found as low as {{convert|300|m|ft|-2|abbr=on}} and as high as {{convert|1800|m|ft|-2|abbr=on}}.<ref name=EOPA-BOW/><ref name=Ridgely2/><ref>{{cite journal |last=Ridgely |first=Robert S. |last2=Robbins |first2=Mark B. |date=1988 |title=''Pyrrhura orcesi'', a new parakeet from southwestern Ecuador, with systematic notes on the ''P. melanura'' complex  |url=https://sora.unm.edu/sites/default/files/journals/wilson/v100n02/p0173-p0182.pdf |journal=Wilson Bulletin |volume=100 |issue=2 |pages=173–343 |access-date=March 12, 2023 }}</ref><ref name=IUCN/>

==Behavior==
===Movement===

The El Oro parakeet is not known to have any pattern of movements.<ref name=EOPA-BOW/>

===Feeding===

The El Oro parakeet feeds on fruit and berries; some known sources are figs (''[[Ficus]]''), ''[[Heliocarpus]]'', ''[[Hieronyma]]'', and ''[[Cecropia]]''. It typically forages in flocks of up to about 15 individuals but flocks as large as 60 have been observed.<ref name=EOPA-BOW/><ref name=Ridgely2/>

===Breeding===

The El Oro parakeet's breeding season appears to span from March to July. It nests in natural tree cavities that have been noted as high as {{convert|24|m|ft|sigfig=1|abbr=on}} above the ground. It uses a variety of trees but ''[[Dacryodes|Dacroide peruviana]]'' appears to be preferred. It also uses nest boxes provided by Buenaventura Reserve.<ref name=EOPA-BOW/><ref name=IUCN/> El Oro parakeets breed cooperatively, a breeding pair being accompanied by up to six helpers.<ref>{{cite journal | first1 = Nadine | last1 = Klauke| first2 = Gernot | last2 = Segelbacher | first3 = H.M. | last3 = Schaefer | title = Reproductive success depends on the quality of helpers in the endangered, cooperative El Oro parakeet (''Pyrrhura orcesi'') | journal = Molecular Ecology | volume = 22 | issue = 7 | date = April 2013 | pages = 2011–2027 |doi=10.1111/mec.12219| pmid = 23397908| s2cid = 41518196| doi-access = free }}</ref>

{{birdsong|url=https://xeno-canto.org/species/Pyrrhura-orcesi |species=El Oro parakeet}}

===Vocalization===

The El Oro parakeet's most common call is "a series of grating notes with a rather hoarse quality, e.g. "crreeet crreeet crreeet" " that is given both from a perch and in flight. Perched birds also call with single "chuk" or "krrr" notes, though perched birds are often silent. Members of a flock "call frequently and simultaneously, producing a noisy chattering."<ref name=EOPA-BOW/>

==Status==

The [[IUCN]] originally assessed the El Oro parakeet as Vulneralble but since 2000 has listed it as Endangered. It has a very limited range and an estimated population of fewer than 1000 mature individuals. "Remaining habitat is fragmented, and both range and population are thought to be declining."<ref name=IUCN/> The Buenaventura Reserve was created in 1999 by Fundación de Conservación Jocotoco to conserve this species and the [[El Oro tapaculo]] (''Scytalopus robbinsi''). It encompasses about {{convert|4000|ha|sqmi|abbr=on}} of [[cloudforest]] in which the Fundación maintains a network of nest boxes.<ref>{{cite web |url=https://abcbirds.org/abc-birding-buenaventura-reserve-ecuador/ |title=ABC Birding: Buenaventura Reserve, Ecuador |author=<!--Not stated--> |date=January 5, 2023 |publisher=American Bird Conservancy |access-date=March 12, 2023 }}</ref> About 75% of the El Oro parakeet's population is found there and nearby.<ref name=IUCN/>
